text: The surgeon begins a robotic anterior resection for sigmoid cancer, starting with a medial to lateral approach. The right pelvic peritoneum and appendix are drawn up into the tumor. The surgeon traces along the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), identifying bulky lymph nodes at the junction with the aorta, aiming to excise them while preserving the surrounding nerves.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) while preserving surrounding nerves during a medial dissection. The superior hemorrhoidal artery is identified distal to the left colic artery take-off and is sealed using a vessel sealer. The ureter is visible, and the surgeon proceeds to take the left colic artery, completing the medial to lateral dissection.

The surgeon identifies the molybdenum fistula to the bladder and the appendix, deciding to perform an en bloc resection. The surgeon then proceeds with lateral dissection before addressing the fistula.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection, clearly visualizing the ureter, and proceeds towards the area of adherence to the fistula. The junction between the colon and bladder is marked, and the surgeon begins to incise into the bladder. The incision reveals the mucosa of the bladder, exposing a large exophytic frond-like tumor projecting into the bladder, leading to some fragmentation of the tumor.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the tumor, visible as a large frond-like mass at the dome of the bladder. The dissection proceeds down the right and left sides, with the ureter safely distant, allowing for cauterization. The posterior wall of the bladder is exposed, and the surgeon continues to work around the inferior posterior margin. The Foley catheter and ureteral stents are visible in the background, providing reassurance of the ureter's location.

The surgeon performs a distal division of the bowel and transects the proximal mesentery, avoiding a small diverticulum by going proximal to it. A fan and steel incision is made to extract the specimen, followed by cleaning up tumor debris and repairing the bladder in an open fashion.

The surgeon mobilizes the bladder completely below the pubic tubercle, revealing a direct hernia on the right. The specimen is extracted through a fan and steel incision, and the bladder is repaired using layers of PDS and serosal silk sutures. The surgeon returns to robotic assistance to perform a sononastomosis, suturing the stapled ends together, creating a proctotomy and colotomy, and sewing the inner back row towards the patient's left. ICG is checked for perfusion, and the surgeon prefers a sewn anastomosis over using an EEA stapler.

In these cases where a large bladder repair is done, I will leave a Foley catheter, I will leave a pelvic drain, and then typically about five to seven days later, I will get a retrograde cystogram and check the bladder integrity before I remove the Foley catheter. The drain is left in place in the event that high output is noted and it could signify a bladder leak before you even do your study, but also in the event that the cystogram proves that there is an occult leak that you didn't know about, you already have a drain in place and you're fully in place that you would just leave for a little bit longer, presuming you didn't need to do any further surgical repair. Although I did have urology involved in the case to do the stents for this procedure, I did the bladder repair myself. It's really no different than sewing bowel together, honestly. So we'll come around the anteriorly and I do do an imbrogating canal suture for this.

The surgeon demonstrates a wide open anastomosis, with the inner layer sutures on the outer row being crossed and tied together. The original suture used to sew the staple lines is run back over the top to imbricate the anterior row, providing additional security. A leak test is performed on the bladder using methylene blue in saline, with no leakage observed. The stents are removed, and the Foley catheter remains in place.
